data.title: UL Solutions: Test Business Testing Its Highs
data.description:
Shares of UL Solutions Inc. (ULS) have revisited all-time highs around the $90 mark again after investors gave their vote of confidence on a relatively larger deal. The deal adds about 6-7% to pro forma sales, with the purchase price taking place at a meaningful discount compared to its valuation.

This is to be applauded, and while I like the deal and pro forma leverage will be very manageable, with solid growth seen in 2026, the reality is that expectations remain very high again as shares have been retesting their highs.

Trading around 45 times realistic earnings going forward, I am erring on the side of caution here, as the premium positioning is awarded more than premium valuations.

I like this move (similar to other deals we cover at Value In Corporate Events), with UL Solutions adding quickly to its acquisition track record here.

Buying Part Of Eurofins

UL Solutions has reached an agreement to acquire the electrical and electronics (E&E) business of Eurofins Scientific SE. This includes the MET Labs certification mark, with the transaction designed to expand testing, inspection, and certification businesses for electrical safety and connected products.

These activities are located across the globe, with a focus on Europe, with capabilities including electromagnetic capability, wireless, electrical safety, simulation, performance testing, product certification, etc. The activities include 44 laboratories and over 1,000 accreditations.

The purchase price is set at EUR 575 million, or approximately $670 million in dollar terms, in part payable from proceeds of the sale of the Employee Health & Safety software business. In fact, the proceeds of the sale (which closed on April 1) will fund about 30% of this deal, according to the press release.

The business is seen generating $200 million in sales in 2026, suggesting that just over 3.3 times sales multiple has been paid. The deal is valued at 14.5 times EBITDA; that multiple, however, includes non-specific synergies to be achieved in year three. That implies that the EBITDA contribution is seen at $46 million, again including synergies. This suggests that activities post margins around 23% of sales.

The deal is seen as closing in the fourth quarter and as accretive to adjusted earnings per share in year one post-closing, the extent of which has not been quantified.

A Cheaper Deal

UL Solutions reported its 2025 results in February, as the company reported resilient results. Both reported and organic sales grew by a little over 6% to $3.05 billion. This is driven by a $1.3 billion industrial segment and an equally large consumer segment, although the adjusted EBITDA margins in the mid-thirties reported by the industrial segment come in at nearly twice the margins reported by the consumer segment. This is complemented by a smaller, near $400 million software and advisory business, one carrying lower margins as well.

The real strength was seen in margins. Adjusted margins rose by no less than 3% to just over a quarter of sales, or $792 million in dollar terms. That was a sizeable number in relation to a tiny net debt load of just $200 million. Adjusted earnings were reported at $1.99 per share, which, however, includes an adjustment of $0.23 per share for stock-based compensation expenses, with realistic earnings otherwise seen around $1.80 per share. This makes it evident that valuations around the $90 mark are sky-high at around 50 times earnings.

With 204 million shares trading near the $90 mark, the company is valued at around $18.4 billion, a valuation that remains largely the same amidst a minimal net debt load, with the enterprise valuation seen at $18.6 billion. This values the business at around 6 times sales and about 23 times adjusted EBITDA. In that sense, the purchase of the Eurofins assets comes at a deep discount, with the applied sales multiple being more than 40% lower and the EBITDA multiple seen over a third lower.

Pro forma net debt would jump to $870 million, ahead of a smaller divestment, no issue at all, with leverage ratios largely seen around 1 times. The $200 million revenue contribution adds about 6-7% to pro forma sales growth, which is ahead of the anticipated growth seen in 2026. The company guided for mid-single-digit organic growth rates in 2026, with EBITDA margins rising further to 26.5-27.0%.

That suggests about a $50-$60 million EBITDA contribution alone, which, given little depreciation intensity, could boost earnings by twenty cents alone, ahead of a contribution of the M&A deal, with further margin gains seen driven by cost reduction efforts internally.

Hence, I am happy to work with realistic earnings numbers just north of $2 per share for 2026.

What Now?

The reality is that even a $2 earnings per share number for 2026 translates into very demanding multiples, as shares by this logic trade at 45 times earnings, while leverage is very much reported around 1 times, and likely less.

In fact, a $5 per share jump in response to the deal, or about 6% in percentage terms, adds about a billion in value on the back of a $670 million deal. While some discount has been paid for the assets, and I appreciate that investors like that, the reaction in isolation seems a bit of an overreaction, although it clearly shows the continued ambitions of the business here.

While this is greatly appreciated, I continue to point to the valuations, similar to the conclusion drawn in November, when I argued that premium valuations did not pass my test. The safety & certification business is extremely well positioned, with tests used for consumer safety, sustainability, dangerous goods, regulatory reporting, and others.

The company went public about two years ago, as shares were sold at $28 per share at the time, as valuations looked largely reasonable. While the company has seen solid growth ever since, mostly valuations have been exploding higher, with shares largely tripling ever since, pushing up valuation multiple expectations a great deal.

And Now?

With UL Solutions having ended 2025 on a solid note and announcing a larger and relatively cheaper deal in April, the company continues to perform in a steady fashion, with shares retesting the highs since the public offering. While all signs are on green for the business, the reality remains the issue of the premium expectations, which prevent me from getting involved, even as I like the recent move made.

However, shares at times show some volatility, this just being a $70 stock in February, at a time when multiples came down to earnings multiples in the mid-thirties. Those remain very premium multiples but offer an earnings yield of nearly a point higher, at near 3%, which looks a lot different from a low 2% earnings yield that shares offer today.

Hence, shares remain worthwhile to keep a close eye upon, and despite a solid deal, I am not willing to chase the shares here.

data.title: Energy Vault: From Breakthrough To Real EBITDA
data.description:
Energy Vault Holdings, Inc.'s (NRGV) situation has changed and is even better than I was expecting. When I first analyzed the stock, we had Q3 2025 results and the company was getting out of startup phase. Revenue then jumped more than 2700% YoY, number might seem high, but it was just low comparable, backlog reached $920 million, and Asset Vault platform was introduced as primary step towards recurring revenue model.

However, at the time, it was just a transition story. The majority of my thesis was built on whether the management will be able to make this happen. We did not have profitability and execution risk was very high. Now I think we are in execution phase. Q4 showed first positive EBITDA, backlog jumped to $1.3 billion and most importantly Asset Vault is generating actual cash flows.

What Actually Changed?

Looking at 2025 results, the first thing that comes to my mind is potential. Annual revenue reached $203.7 million, that is 340% growth, and Q4 alone generated $153 million, the numbers that I believe are good for this company. Here I think it is important to understand the context because this growth is not organic in classical sense. Majority of this jump comes from the recognition of delayed projects. Also, we can add that it comes from the company starting to work on contracts normally. In other words, execution in my opinion seems to be catching up.

I believe that the margins are good as well. Gross margin was 23.6%, and it surpassed guidance of 14-16%. This shows not only better project mix, but that Asset Vault model is starting show effect on profitability. For the first time in Q4 EBITDA was positive ($9.8 million), but sadly, year still ended up negatively. I believe that the trend is clear, operating leverage is finally materializing. Revenue is growing faster than costs and I actually think that we have first real signal that the model could work.

Backlog increased to $1.3 billion, from $920 million which was before. I think that it is not about the size of this backlog but structure. Because now it is not a pipeline, but long-term contracts and Asset Vault based contracts, which I think have better value and visibility.

Cash position of $103 million together with convertible bonds and OIC fund mean that survival risk is practically gone. At least this is my opinion. But the risk now comes from capital structure as it got more complex and potentially expensive to shareholders.

Asset Vault

Overall Energy Vault investment story changed due to Asset Vault. In Q3 we have seen idea with first pilot tests. Now we have a working model. Calistoga and Cross Trails projects are now generating around $10 million annual EBITDA. While the numbers are not big, but I think they are critical as this is the evidence that the model is working. Then we have Stoney Creek project in Australia, which potentially could generate another $20 million EBITDA, while SOSA and EBOR pipeline shows that this shouldn't be a one-off.

The company is moving from EPC contractor, which earns one-off revenue from it's projects, to infrastructure operator. And that is good, because they can generate recurring EBITDA from their assets. To me it changed valuation as well. I believe we should not look at revenue multiple. The company is becoming infrastructure player, so it should be valued by EBITDA and long-term cash flow.

Another interesting and important signal I think is their scale. Throughout 12 months, contracted power increased from 65 MW to 540 MW. As I said, we are now getting a lot of evidence that the platform can be scaled.

What We Should Expect In 2026

Looking into 2026, my main concern is how fast they are going to convert into real cash flow and how the market will start to value them. I have to admit and inform, that I am planning on opening a position again, after getting hit on my stop-loss, so I might be biased. Management guidance shows $225-300 million revenue, but this is another nuance - part of Asset Vault projects will not be reflected in GAAP revenue. That means, that the market could overreact the difference between reported and economic revenue.

Currently, I think that the best catalyst is Japan contract. Acquisition of 850 MW portfolio is immediately expanding the scale and potentially could make more than $180 million EBITDA. Also it adds diversification, because projects are not just in U.S. and Australia.

Additionally we have also a new positioning angle. Partnerships with Crusoe (data centers) and Peak Energy (they make batteries), gives me a feeling that the company does not try to be only a storage player, but become bigger part of infrastructure.

All in all, for 2026, we should expect to see Asset Vault moving and becoming sustainable. That should be clear in a few quarters to be fair. Japan contract and overall backlog is very good, but I believe that the market will react more to execution than headlines. I am always searching for stocks to jump into before they re-rate, and first profitable quarter might lead to profitable full year. Just keep in mind, if you follow same strategy, it can backfire.

Risks

The biggest risk did not go anywhere and the same as a catalyst it is execution. Business still stands and being built on a few big projects and I believe that the risk comes from delays or technical problems. These could hit revenue and cash flow directly. Still, the model itself is not very diversified and one contract can make an impact.

While Asset Vault model looks good from EBITDA, it needs capital. Convertible bonds and preferred equity solves short-term problem, but also shows us that the growth is being financed and not generated by operations. This leads me to believe that even if I like them, margins might be fragile. Q4 showed clear improvement, but still question is whether it can be repeated.

Technology risk with G-Vault and H-Vault dilutes my conviction a bit, because those projects are not proven commercially. My whole thesis on them is based on assumptions. While at the same time the company might get pressured competition from Tesla or Fluence, which have scale and financial resources and one of them even has hype behind it. All of which sadly Energy Vault does not have.

Valuation

As I mentioned before I am biased. We cannot really use classical P/E or PEG because the company is just transitioning to profitability. So, I tried using P/S ratio. At current capitalization of $560 million and revenue of $203.7 million in 2025, current P/S is around 2.7x. If I add guidance in 2026 of $262.5 million, then ratio is 2.1x.

Management at the same time is giving hints of 30% revenue growth. Combining these two numbers we get a ratio between price and growth, which I can say is lower than some of the peers, but at the same time it seems fairly priced as of now. If we speculate let's say and use Forward P/S that I mentioned plus growth of 30%, we get GARP opportunity. Ratio of 2.1/30= 0.07. Then the stock seems to be very cheap and to be fair it is one of the cheapest companies by this metric that I have in my watch list.

Summary

Energy Vault in 5 months since my article transitioned from promises to actual execution. Revenue jumped quite strongly, margins are now better, and Q4 showed first positive EBITDA. Now I have even more confidence, even though, the stock dropped 20% since my first buy rating. Even though we still have execution and capital intensity risks, valuation seems to be attractive to me. I will be looking for entry myself, so my NRGV rating is maintained at Buy.

data.title: Dutch Bros: A High-Growth Coffee Chain Navigating Uncertain Waters
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Dutch Bros (BROS), I highlighted their strong growth and significant expansion plans, standing to benefit from their potential to capture market share from more mature peers despite macro headwinds.

Following a solid quarter and year, BROS remains a Buy, backed by a more attractive valuation that already seems to imply a very solid margin of safety if the company manages to navigate the current macro pressure well.

Strong Growth Continues

BROS reported yet another solid quarter and year, beating the market’s top- and bottom-line estimates, with 27.9% YoY total revenue growth in 2025, opening 154 new shops, advancing their profitability pivot, and delivering their 19th consecutive year of positive same shop sales growth, which remains impressive given the current environment marked by consumer weakness.

For 2026, BROS sees at least 181 new shop openings, with revenue between $2 billion and $2.03 billion, 3% to 5% growth in same shop sales growth, $355 million to $365 million in Adj. EBITDA (vs. $302.55 million in 2025) and $270 million to $290 million in CAPEX, a significant increase from the $241.13 million seen in 2025, highlighting a major increase in top- and bottom-line as they are advancing on their path to 2029 stores by 2029. As a note, this guidance came shortly after the drop in coffee prices and before the Supreme Court’s tariff decision.

Financially, based on BROS' latest report, we continue to see a very solid position, with current assets above their current liabilities and a very manageable amount of debt that was even reduced YoY, with the asset-light business model (leasing instead of owning properties) offering a solid advantage for a company aiming to grow so much, providing flexibility in opening/closing stores much faster than if they were to own the property.

BROS is expected to release their next earnings report on May 7, with expectations of $0.16 in Normalized EPS and $449.24 million in revenue. Given the current environment, I will pay close attention to potential changes in their plans, as the company can see much more pressure on the consumer that can affect their expansion in the near-term, which the market may already be pricing in to some degree given the drop seen YTD. I also wouldn’t be surprised to see them update the guidance a bit given the change in tariffs and coffee prices, which are positive.

Macro Risks Are Rising Fast

Unlike McDonald’s (MCD) or Domino’s (DPZ), which have major supply chains and significant funds available to allow them to adapt to the current consumer weakness while still delivering strong results, BROS is significantly smaller, and issuing stock would only fuel the already high amount of short betting since it would dilute in the near-term.

This makes the company much more exposed to the current weakness, especially given the massive jump seen in coffee prices not long ago, which remain very elevated despite crashing recently. This can make it harder for BROS to continue expanding, offer the same high level of quality and keep developing their margins at the same time. Not only that, but we saw Treasury Secretary Bessent reigniting the market’s tariff fears very recently:

We had a setback at the Supreme Court in terms of the tariff policy, but we will be implementing or conducting Section 301 studies, so the tariffs could be back in place at the previous level by beginning of July

Meanwhile, Dutch Bros’ CFO also highlighted something very important about the delay between coffee prices and the impact on their P&L during their Q4 Earnings Call:

With coffee costs remaining elevated throughout 2025, the impact increased throughout the year and will have a continued impact into 2026. Given our inventory turns, any change in coffee prices, including the related P&L impact, typically lags by 2 to 3 quarters. The midpoint of our full year 2026 guidance contemplates approximately 80 basis points of total COGS pressure. Included in this is approximately 200 basis points of total COGS pressure in Q1 2026, with that pressure stepping down throughout the year.

A new wave of tariffs would be the last thing this company needs, as the Iran conflict can lead to very significant levels of inflation already - regardless of when the conflict ends at this point - with the IMF recently warning that the world is already heading towards their “more adverse” scenario, which implies a 2.5% growth rate in global GDP this year, with potential of a recession in case we see it last for longer.

At this point, we’ve likely hit the point of no return in terms of an inflationary shock, as the current war premiums in oil and other vital commodities like fertilizers can last for much longer than the conflict itself given the significant damage already dealt to the region’s energy infrastructure, which can limit supply at a time when countries would have to replenish their strategic reserves.

As a result, the Fed and other central banks can be pushed into a position where they either have to reconsider their rate cuts or even hike them, in an effort to combat the inflation that would result from this, which can make treasury yields more attractive since the market would require better rewards for the risks it’s taking, potentially putting more pressure on valuations.

This would come alongside higher borrowing costs for the company and their partners, while consumers would face even more pressure at a time when many consumer-related companies have already been reporting issues for years, with significant trade-downs and pressure on discretionary budgets that can affect BROS quite a bit - making their performance in recent years even more impressive. One BofA analyst also considers that the challenging consumer environment will require a strong ability to offset rising costs when it comes to this industry, with demand trends expected to remain weak, for now.

Something else the market is paying attention to is GLP-1 adoption, which is estimated to cause a hit of about 8% in fast-food and limited-service restaurants (including coffee shops). I highlighted before why I don’t consider it to be such a major hit as it may seem - despite being a huge growth catalyst for companies like Novo Nordisk (NVO) - while Morgan Stanley recently estimated that the adoption rate could reach about 15% of the US population by 2035, which further reinforces my beliefs that this is something that companies like BROS can easily offset through pricing or cost savings throughout the decade.

Risks Reiterated

Although BROS has been able to develop very well in recent years and their profitability inflection is advancing well, that doesn’t mean they haven’t been affected by the consumer pressure seen in recent years, while the environment can rapidly deteriorate from this point.

Meanwhile, this remains a very competitive market, while broader macro pressure places this relatively small company in a tough position, where they have to skillfully balance significant growth, margin improvements, cost pressure, and potential demand issues at the same time, which will require very strong execution from their management.

Valuation

For BROS’ DCF to Equity valuation, assuming no changes in net borrowings given their strong balance sheet, I’ll assume their OCF can grow by 19% in 2026 (same as the Adj. EBITDA), reaching ~$351.70 million at midpoint while the CAPEX would get to a midpoint of $280 million, meaning about $71.70 million in FCF in 2026.

I’ll continue to assume they can grow the OCF at a 30% CAGR until 2030 (similar to the market’s EPS expectations) while the CAPEX would grow at a 5% CAGR during this time (which is already elevated given the asset-light business model), further supporting their significant growth, which would then be followed by a 17.5% CAGR in FCF for the subsequent 5 years given their significant expansion potential domestically and internationally, with a 3% terminal growth rate given the potential to expand in higher-growth regions, as well as a 12% discount rate to demand a strong margin of safety, with 177.92 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $8.72 billion, for an intrinsic value of $49.02 per share, which is very close to the current $52.11 at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

BROS remains a Buy, backed by an attractive valuation and strong long-term potential to grow and expand domestically and even internationally, with the price already pricing in high levels of risk.

In other words, if the company manages to expand and navigate the current environment, the current price already implies a solid margin of safety through the high discount rate, while potential macro improvements can result in even better numbers.

data.title: Universal Health Services Is A Huge Bargain At 8x Earnings Vs HCA At 16x
data.description:
Summary

Universal Health Services (UHS), an operator of acute care hospitals and mental health facilities with earnings roughly evenly split between the two divisions, has seen its stock price struggle of late.

We’ve discussed UHS several times: we purchased it at prices we like during Covid and it remains a solid Compounder: EPS has grown by 15.0% a year over the past five years and is up 14.1% over the past 11 years.

Below is an updated cap structure.

Leverage is quite low and the company has been buying back significant amounts of stock, 5% last year alone.

Fourth Quarter Results

For the fourth quarter, earnings were solid but missed expectations. Revenue grew 9.1% but was a small 0.5% point miss.

EPS jumped 20% but was a 3c miss ($5.88 vs $5.91 estimates). The miss here, while infinitesimally small (a 0.5% miss), coupled with concerns regarding staffing rules in California/ACA impacts, has caused a 25% hit the shares (from its February peak).

We view this is a dramatic overreaction.

Here is UHS on an EV/EBITDA compared to HCA Inc (HCA), its best peer.

Despite good numbers, UHS is now trading at a decade low valuation at 5.7x 2026 EBITDA. Meanwhile, HCA has rated to a near record HIGH at 10.1x (UHS in orange above and HCA in green).

For the full year 2025, UHS' revenue grew 9% and EPS by an impressive 31%. While comps were “easy” given falling nursing costs, higher pricing on the Behavioral health side and higher admissions, the long term track record remains solid, if a bit lumpy.

Guidance seemed the biggest concern to investors as growth is slowing. In our view it is simply "normalizing" from excessive growth levels after a banner 2025.

For 2026, despite the vast difference in valuation, UHS is expected to grow faster than HCA. At the midpoint, revenue, EBITDA and EPS are anticipated to grow at UHS 7%, 5% and 9% respectively in 2026. HCA estimates show growth at 4%, 3% and 7.5% respectively.

More on this in a minute.

KPIs

Operationally speaking, here is some good data for the year 2025.

Admissions for the year were up on the Acute Care Hospital side by 3.8% but were flat in Q4. Lower tourism in Las Vegas, a large market for UHS, hampered the fourth quarter in terms of admissions.

Revenue per admission (prices in essence but is affected by mix) grew 5.4% in Q4. With one extra hospital in 2025, total revenue was up 11%. We credit management with controlling costs well; EBITDA margins in the Acute Hospital segment grew 150 bps to 15.8% for the year.

On the Behavioral Health side, admissions were flat for the year but grew 1.5% on a same store basis in Q4. Kind of the opposite compared to the hospital side. Revenue per patient was up nicely, 5.6% in Q4. With a labor shortage continuing in this segment, EBITDA grew a hair less than revenue. Management also is hiring as they push growth in this segment in 2026.

Guidance

Below are UHS guidance numbers for 2026.

Overall, UHS had a solid year with EPS growth of 31%; 5% of which was due to share buybacks. Speaking of, the company has authorized buybacks of $1.425 billion, or 12% of the float.

That could offer another 4-6% tailwind to EPS growth in 2026. And should provide a lot of technical support for the equity.

As for “weak” guidance, volume growth is anticipated to be 2-3% in both segments with pricing up 2-4%. So, revenue is expected to grow this year between 6-8%, EBITDA up 2-8% and EPS up 4-13%.

Some wide ranges here but pretty good at the midpoint. Management has a solid record of beating estimates too.

The biggest impact to 2026 EBITDA is related to consumers losing access to healthcare plans under the health insurance exchanges (ACA subsidies). These subsidies expired at the end of 2025 and seems to be weighing particularly hard on the stock as this year is a “transition year”.

Last year 6% of their Acute care patient admissions were ACA insured patients (and 5% of revenue). This will end up impacting margins as they will continue to be admissions but not likely paying admissions this year. Management discussed a $75 million pre-tax hit on their last call.

On the Behavioral side, California has enacted minimum staffing standards starting June 1, 2026 that will require $35 million in incremental nursing costs in 2026. That will impact them by another $30 million in 2027.

On the AI front, management had this to say:

I certainly believe we, like most, are in the early innings of this AI game. I think our initial efforts have really focused on administrative sort of efforts like within our revenue cycle management. I think Marc alluded to claims appeals and coding. And we've used that, I think, to great effectiveness. I think really, what we're doing and otherwise, I think you also referred to post-discharge activity. So historically, a nurse would make a post-discharge follow-up phone call with a patient to ensure they're being compliant with their medications and their diet and follow-up appointments with physicians. And now we're using an AI agent to make those calls in many cases. And so in both cases, I think we're driving efficiencies. It allows us to reduce headcount. It improves the outcomes as measured by revenue cycle metrics or reduction in readmissions."

With cost controls and help from AI, margins won’t be impacted too much in our opinion. As an aside, UHS owns a stake in Hippocratic AI, a startup that is customer facing. Hippocratic AI can call up to 100,000 patients a day to check on their well being, schedule care, etc. The company is a co-founder of Hippocratic AI and it seems a free option for equity holders.

Forecast

We took a conservative approach in 2026 and are using $23 in EPS (vs $23.58 guidance and $23.37 Street estimates).

Below is our model and forecast.

In 2025, management originally guided to $19.20 in EPS and ended up at $21.74. A huge beat despite the threat to Medicaid Supplemental payments (which are about 20% of EBITDA, and are still a concern in 2028).

For 2026, management guided to $23 million in incremental Medicaid supplemental payments compared to 2025. Increases in state directed payments also appears an offset.

But there is risk that in 2028, the Big Beautiful Bill (BBB) will cut rates on Medicaid patients from the Average Commercial Rate to the Medicare rate (which is lower). According to the sellside, the impact will mean a $200-340 million reduction in EBITDA starting in 2028 and phasing in through 2030.

Some analysts suggest a 200 basis point hit to EBITDA margins.

In our bear case, we projected margins falling to 13% from 15% and a hit to EBITDA of $260 million. While this would phase in over time, we assumed they impacted margins immediately. That would push EPS back to $21 a share (but the reality would be a 3 year phase in give or take).

Street estimates are vastly higher at $27.55 in earnings per share in 2028. At its normal 12-15x multiple, UHS trades well over $300.

Recently the company discussed some of the BBB impacts as per below..

So we've been spending a lot of time in D.C. I honestly think that it's unknown what's going to happen, come '28. We've gotten a lot of positive feedback, though, from moderates on both sides of the aisle that this won't stand as it is today. And they expect something to happen legislatively, probably in the next Congress. But if not this one coming up, then the one after in some way because we'll be able to absorb whatever we have to absorb. We'll adjust our operations. We've proven that we can do that. I think the 80% of the hospitals that are not-for-profit in the U.S., a lot of them are going to have a much harder time. And the folks in D.C. recognize that. So it's a wait and see. I can't really predict what will happen. I just think that it will moderate in some way, and we'll be in the forefront there, and we'll take care of that."

With the stock at $180 and under 8x forward earnings, it seems UHS is pricing in zero growth (which may be true out to 2028 should margin take a hit). But afterward growth should resume especially as demographics support more hospital care. As well, mental health demand continues to grow: there seems little debate that demand is well in excess of supply in the field.

A potential positive for UHS would be a Democratic sweep of Congress this year and not only delaying the BBB impact but also re-instating ACA subsidies. That alone would add $75 million back to pre-tax earnings with EPS edging closer to $25 per share.

As an aside, HCA also will face the issue of losing many exchange insured patients and also faces Medicaid impacts in 2028. It is worse for them in some ways as 10% of their revenue last year was from ACA subsidized patients (vs 5% at UHS). The 2028 cliff is less impactful to HCA based on their sunbelt state geographies, but it remains an issue for them as well.

Valuation

Lots of rough numbers here, but the range of forward EPS for UHS is probably $21 to $31 in 2029-2030 (and aligns with Street estimates today).

At a 12-14x P/E (the long term average) and using $30 in 2030 EPS, we see $360 to $420 as a possibility (up ~100% in 3-4 years).

The bear case, $21 in EPS in 2028, suggests $168 in a couple of years at a trough multiple. The good news is that the stock could trade to an HCA level in 2028 once Medicaid cliff impacts have been resolved. Growth can then resume and long term, their track record is similar to HCA's.

Some scenarios below.

We call it a range of $160 to $390+ in our bull case in 2030.

Conclusion

Looks like a buy. Heads, I don’t lose too much but tails I could win big.

Betting markets imply 85% odds that Dems retake control of the House in this year’s election. The Senate is a toss up with odds a touch over 50% that Democratics regain control. Should Republicans maintain control, ACA subsidies would remain gone. That seems more than priced into the stock already. A Democratic Congressional sweep would be a boon for UHS however, and one not considered by investors today.

There is already a lot of moderate sympathy for what happens to the hospital system should Medicaid supplements get enacted as planned. While negative for UHS, it would be devastating to the thousands of non-profit, lower margin hospitals out there, especially in rural areas.

The valuation differential between HCA and UHS is stark. Both are growing similarly and I like the behavioral side too. Utilization rates are still low and any improvement there along with share buybacks could help lift the stock.

data.title: Marzetti: Volume Growth Nascent, Clear Underweight
data.description:
The Marzetti Company (MZTI) is a manufacturer and marketer of specialty food products in the US. It's home to brands like Sister Schubert's and New York Bakery. You may best know them for having licensed Chick-fil-A sauces in your local grocery store. It offers products at both grocery stores and in 17 of the country's top 30 restaurant chains. The company has been around since 1961 and now has 13 production facilities across seven states. The issue with this company is that volume growth is going to be hard to come by as it fights structural consumer consumption headwinds for their product categories, all the while trading at a level that is not particularly attractive on a forward basis.

Opportunity Set

The company will tell you it has produced an 11% growth CAGR from 1972 to 2025, which is a bit misleading. Sure, Marzetti has deals with Buffalo Wild Wings, Olive Garden, Chick-fil-A, Arby's, and Texas Roadhouse on the restaurant side, but this wide of a view offers little to paint the forward trajectory.

Source: Investor Presentation

To understand this company better, their revenues are split about half retail and half foodservice, with the latter being slightly larger. On a retail mix basis, 38% is frozen bread, 19% is refrigerated dressings, dips, and other, while 43% are shelf-stable dressings, sauces, and croutons. It is difficult, right off the bat, to really feel good about this product mix. The rise of GLP-1s and lower calorie intake consumers puts a strain on conversion at PoS for these types of items. This is where structural headwinds come into play.

There are catalysts on the refrigerated dressing front: there is a new Buffalo Wild Wings sauce (honey garlic) and hot sauce coming out this spring, as well as Zesty Italian Olive Garden-branded dressing this spring. On the new product front, the company is adding "baked bites" under its New York Bakery brand this summer and new dressings under the Marzetti line this spring. Quantifying any of these is difficult, as a flavor here and there or a small product launch will tend to not move the needle.

With a bread and dressing offering, they have laid out a growth strategy to prospective investors: grow OSG% volumes L/MSD% and grow operating margins. For anyone who has spent time covering consumer staples, you'll know that L/MSD% volume growth is a fantastic outcome if it actually happens, but that most consumer staples companies are incapable of driving organic volume growth without points of distribution growth. Most consumer staples companies falter on volume and eke out small revenue gains through M&A and pricing. If there is truly a volume-driven story in consumer staples, that's the name to own.

The problem with Marzetti's strategy is that the volume goals are completely unrealistic, and competition from private labels will only further hinder this company's ability to produce competitive returns for shareholders. If the biggest thing they have going on is adding frozen dinner rolls to their lineup, they're stuck 10 years in the past and are going to be more and more reliant on M&A to hit their goals.

M&A is important to discuss with respect to Marzetti. The company recently announced it is purchasing Bachan, the maker of specialty sauces at a premium price point, for $400 million in cash. The transaction is expected to be accretive to top-line growth and gross margin in year 1 and will close by the end of their fiscal year (which ends in June). Bachan adds very little to the company: $87 million in net revenue in 2025. While that's 4 points of growth over roughly a $2 billion base of total revenue, it's simply a decision they had to make to offset core portfolio volume losses.

Source: Investor Presentation

Two things should be apparent at this point: the company is leaning into dressings and sauces, and there is no clear plan to drive volume growth with this portfolio tilt. Just this past quarter, it was blamed on a difficult comparison for why they had a 3% decline in volume growth. Since last year, the company has introduced several new products and flavors for sauces, but if they're unable to grow, there is little reason to get involved here.

Seldom discussed, but there's an opportunity for MZTI to improve its supply chain. They've been selectively integrating by acquiring things like a sauce and dressing plant in Atlanta (from Winland Foods) as well as closing a sauce and dressing facility in California. This shifting is meant to improve the supply chain portfolio's dynamics and produce tangible cost savings. Growth for MZTI is driven by licensed products and food service chains, so this needs to be humming.

Financial Profile and Valuation

My issue with this company is the lack of core volume growth — organic volume growth is negative. In the most recent quarter, core volume and product mix drove a 1.3% decline. Pricing was 1.4% positive, and with the balance of growth coming from M&A, this business inorganically grew, especially since the bulk of the inorganic growth came from a temporary supply agreement with Winland Foods, whose facility in Atlanta they purchased. For longer-term investors, it's important to note the dividend history here. The company has increased its regular cash dividend for 63 consecutive years, a feat achieved by only 12 other companies in the US. Below is how the yield has trended over the last year - notably increasing to nearly 2.75% as the stock has been a laggard.

Source: Passage AI

The problem I have with this company is they spend more on dividends than they do on CapEx. That's not a great sign. Now, if you're a thirty-year shareholder, you may not care so long as they never cut the dividend; however, this isn't an asset-light business: they are a manufacturer of food products, and that requires facilities and scale if their products are going to scale nationally, let alone internationally one day. This is not responsible capital allocation. Part of the issue is that they cannot find anything more in their current portfolio to invest in. Innovation is light at this company, and that is a management flaw.

The stock is down 20% over the past 5 years, drastically underperforming the market and the XLP, the latter by nearly 40 points. Consumer staples, too, as a subsector, have staged a great rally YTD, up nearly 6.5%, and yet Marzetti is down 13% in the same time frame.

Source: Passage AI

The only thing to be careful about being negative on a company like this is that there is a scarcity value for inefficient companies in SMID-cap consumer staples. What I mean by this is that the large consumer staples companies also have volume issues, especially with the rise of private labels over the last 10-20 years, and M&A is not frowned upon at that level as much as at the SMID-cap level. MZTI does screen as an acquisition target.

Now, there's no guidance from management outside last year's Investor Day framework that I outlined, but the market is assuming M/HSD% growth in EPS for FY26/FY27 EPS estimates of $7.10/$7.44 - this effectively means that the stock on next year's numbers is trading at 20x for pretty lackluster growth. There are better buys out there - especially cross different sectors - for that multiple.

Earnings growth of M/HSD% without context can be quite good. Usually, if a stock can put up one or two turns of multiple expansion per year plus get credit for their earnings growth, all the while clipping a dividend of a few percent, annualized returns are in the ~high-teens % range, which for a consumer staples investor, is a great outcome for what is likely one of the more defensive parts of one's portfolio. Consumer staples companies tend to get a premium valuation relative to other subsectors because of their defensive qualities. At the end of the day, people need to eat, so demand slowdowns are shallower relative to other subsectors in a slowdown.

That's the issue with Marzetti. Eventually, M&A will not be able to bail them out - core volume growth will simply have to increase, and the structural factors at play work against them. That will lead to earnings declines and multiple contraction.

Conclusion

Marzetti seems to be trading at an expensive valuation for volume declines, with the only real growth effort being through bolt-on M&A. This is a tough stock to get excited about in the near term, especially with the larger consumer staples sector having outperformed YTD and the stock having missed out on that rally.

data.title: SOXX's AI Premium Must Be Paid - Near Term Overbought Risks
data.description:
I previously covered VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) in January 2026, discussing its diversified exposure to the multi-year AI spending trends.

SOXX Proves Its AI Beneficiary Status

Similar to SMH, iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) is another semiconductor-focused ETF that has been a beneficiary during the ongoing data center boom

While past performance does not guarantee future results, SOXX has delivered an excellent price return of +210.2% since the launch of ChatGPT by OpenAI (OPENAI) on November 30, 2022.

This is compared to the wider market at +70.3% over the same time period.

1. Robust Exposure To Cloud Super Cycle

Much of SOXX's outperformance is attributed to their somewhat balanced weightage for their top few holdings, such as the top holding at an 8.4% weightage compared to the top 5 averages at ~7.1%.

This strategy allows the ETF to enjoy a diversified exposure into the peaks and troughs of the ongoing AI boom, as observed in NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) in H1'24/Q2'25, Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO) in December 2024/Q2'25, and Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) in H2'25.

Most importantly, SOXX's top holdings are focused on the winners of the AI race, namely:

Nvidia

As the general AI accelerator market leader, NVDA has delivered excellent data center revenue growth, from $14.99 billion in FY2023 to $193.72 billion in FY2026, expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +134.6%.

This is while hinting at "$1 trillion plus of Blackwell plus Rubin" revenues at the end of 2027, up from the prior guidance of $500 billion, with it underscoring the durable data center spending trends entering 2026/2027.

Broadcom

Broadcom has emerged as the custom AI accelerator market leader, with the robust hyperscaler demand already observed in their AI semiconductor revenue growth from $4.2 billion in FY2023 (15% of FY2023 semiconductor revenues) to an annualized sum of $33.6 billion in FQ1'26, expanding at a 2Y CAGR of +182.8%.

The management has also guided an excellent "AI revenue from chips, just chips, in excess of $100 billion in 2027," with it implying an excellent growth profile at a 2Y CAGR of +123.6% from FY2025 levels of $20 billion.

This development is unsurprising indeed, given Broadcom's growing hyperscaler base through Alphabet Inc.'s (GOOG) TPU, ByteDance (BDNCE), Meta Platforms, Inc.'s (META) MTIA, an unnamed customer who previously ordered "$10 billion of orders of AI racks," OpenAI, and two more hyperscalers in development stages.

This is on top of Anthropic (ANTHRO) also signing multiple gigawatts of GOOG's next-generation TPU capacity, as similarly observed with META, with these developments likely allowing Broadcom to raise their FY2027 guidance beyond the prior number of $100 billion.

Micron

With "AI workloads requiring large amounts of memory... AI servers and enterprise environments require far more memory per system than consumer devices," MU has been able to successfully monetize their DRAM and NAND market leadership while delivering a much-needed reversal from the prior memory destruction in 2023.

The same has been embedded in its excellent revenue growth from $15.54 billion in FY2023 to the estimated FY2026 at $104.5 billion (H1'25 at $37.5 billion, the FQ3'26 guidance at $33.5 billion, and the FQ4'26 assumption of $33.5 billion), expanding at an estimated 3Y CAGR of +88.7%.

Combined with their ongoing capacity expansions in numerous locations, the hyperscalers' demand gated by supply, and the ongoing price hikes, it is unsurprising that the consensus already expects MU to deliver an outsized top-line growth in a CAGR of +61.1% through FY2028.

Advanced Micro Devices

This is not forgetting the two other stocks in the top 5 holdings, including Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), which already reported robust data center revenue growth from $6.04 billion in FY2022 to $16.6 billion in FY2025, expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +40%.

Thanks to its diversified growth prospects across the embedded semiconductor (Microsoft's Xbox) and x86 CPU end market, it is unsurprising that the management has offered an ambitious Analyst Day 2025 revenue growth target at a CAGR of +35% and adj EPS growth at +40% over the next few years.

Marvell (MRVL)

As the designer for Amazon's and Microsoft's custom AI chips, Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) has also experienced robust data center revenue growth, from $2.4 billion in FY2023 to $6.09 billion in FY2026, expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +36.4%.

With AMZN already guiding outsized CapEx of $200 billion in FY2026 (+55.8% YoY) and Microsoft announcing numerous data center-related projects over the past few months, I am of the opinion that MRVL is well positioned to deliver their prior FY2027 data center revenue growth guidance by +40% YoY and FY2028 by +50% YoY.

2. Growth Premium Already Embedded

As a result of their holdings' robust, multi-year growth prospects, I can understand why SOXX has been able to enjoy an outsized price rally since the March 2026 bottom, as similarly observed in the wider market and its semiconductor-focused ETFs in varying degrees.

Beyond the top five holdings, readers may also want to note the ETF's strategic focus on US-based stocks, allowing them to mitigate the risks from future geopolitical and/or tariff issues.

This is on top of the benefit of SOXX's elevated exposure to the leading fabless names at 76.1%, with it mitigating the free cash flow headwinds from the foundries' likely to be elevated CapEx trends.

The +29.8% rally over the past two weeks is also why SOXX appears to be somewhat inflated at a P/E of 43.73x, compared to the wider market at 39.08x.

Even then, I am of the opinion that the ETF remains a Great Buy, since its top five holdings continue to trade near to their 5Y P/E means, including:

  • Advanced Micro Devices at a FWD P/E of 38.13x against the 5Y mean of 37.48x and
  • Marvell at 35.01x/36.45x,

aside from:

  • Nvidia at the currently discounted P/E of 23.66x against the 5Y mean of 44.63x and
  • Micron at 8.06x/74.46x,

along with Broadcom's current premium at 33.41x/25.74x, respectively.

Combined with the holdings' high double/triple-digit top/bottom-line growth prospects over the next few years, I believe that SOXX may deliver robust capital appreciation beyond the ongoing rally since the April 2025 bottom.

This is especially since the prior AI pessimism has already been exhausted during the worst of the March 2026 selloff, as the market enters the Q1'26 earnings season afresh in April 2026.

SOXX ETF Is A Buy After A Dip

For now, SOXX is retesting a new high at the $401s after the recent breakout, with it remaining to be seen if the upward momentum may be sustained after the nearly vertical rally.

This is especially since SOXX has been overbought, as observed in the elevated RSIs, with the uncertain macroeconomic and geopolitical environment potentially triggering a profit-taking cadence at current inflated levels.

This is especially since the Iran conflict is still developing. Pending an actual resolution and the consistent flow in the global oil/gas supply, I believe that the inflationary pressure may worsen from current levels, with March 2026's somewhat muted numbers likely to give way to worse numbers in April 2026.

This is especially since the broader chip supply chain constraint may negate the promising global chip sales cadence observed in February 2026, pending the foundries' further capacity expansions.

With market analysts already expecting the Fed to hold interest rates steady in the upcoming FOMC meeting, my initial Buy rating comes with the caveat that interested investors wait for a moderate correction before adding, preferably between the January/February 2026 tops of $360 and the 50-day moving averages of $250s.

Those entry points may offer an improved margin of safety while allowing investors to partake in the structurally robust, multi-year AI supercycle.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: The Fortress Has Become A Waiting Room
data.description:
Introduction

I last covered Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.A) (BRK.B) back in August with a dose of skepticism on the back of the potential Warren Buffett premium eroding. Since the company’s stock has only appreciated 1.94% since Buffett officially retired, I believe that my rating was on point. Now under new CEO Greg Abel, I want to dive into the company’s path forward and whether it has an undervaluation thesis.

Current Dynamics

The biggest dynamic has been Greg Abel assuming the CEO position while Warren's son Howard Buffett assumed the position of Non-Executive Chairman, a role that will be tailored to keep the same corporate culture and prevent short-term institutional decay. The transition has introduced a more institutionalized and team-oriented management style, unlike the historical style of Buffett, which Abel has diverted from while signing a collaborative approach and delegating significant oversight.

In his inaugural letter, Abel also diverted from the folksy and aphoristic wit of his predecessor with a more direct and conservative narrative with a focus on operation. Abel has emphasized three core pillars: stewardship, capital discipline, and the maintenance of the fortress balance sheet. Abel has already demonstrated a more active, albeit still decentralized, management style, asking tougher questions regarding operational efficiency and capital returns. This has been evident in his public critique of BNSF Railway and the utilities under Berkshire Hathaway Energy.

Looking at the broad numbers for 2025, operating earnings have declined by 6.2% to $44.5B, which was largely due to the normalization of the insurance cycle and specific headwinds in the energy and railroad segments. The decline in insurance underwriting earnings was majorly pronounced in Q4, where results fell by nearly 30%, with the weakness driven by a combination of higher catastrophe losses and a deceleration in the pricing power that had boosted margins in 2023 and 2024. Though the manufacturing service and retailing segment showed resilience, thanks to brands like See’s Candies and Dairy Queen.

On the cash front, it remains one of the biggest sources of contemplation, as it now stands at $373.3B at year-end. The level of liquidity is unprecedented, which represents nearly 40% of the firm’s total market capitalization. The bulk of this position is deployed in short-term US treasury bills totaling $321.4B. This reserve is based on two major dynamics that have been noticed by Buffett since the COVID pandemic: the lack of attractive large-scale acquisition opportunities and a cautious view on the broader market valuations. By hoarding such a high level of cash, Berkshire is positioned to act as a lender of last resort or the potential buyer of first choice should a major correction occur.

On the portfolio side, the cash that was diverted away from the Apple (AAPL) stake has been partially redeployed into Alphabet (GOOG) as Berkshire revealed a massive new entry into the firm in Q3 of 2025 as it purchased 17.8MM shares valued at around $4.3B. The investment team viewed Alphabet as a premier play on the integration of GenAI into existing platform ecosystems. I believe that it is the right move, especially as it trimmed the position of Apple and Amazon to hedge an all-around bet on the new tech wave. Meaning that the firm Berkshire is recalibrating its tech exposure toward platform-level AI dominance rather than consumer hardware.

On the industrial front, there are massive structural challenges facing Greg Abel. BNSF has underperformed its primary rival, Union Pacific (UNP), in terms of operating ratio and profitability. The internal struggle has now been compounded by a massive threat that is the proposed mega-merger between UNP and Norfolk Southern (NSC). This would create the first truly transcontinental railroad in U.S. history, with a network spanning over 52,000 miles across 43 states. This would essentially put BNSF at a structural disadvantage.

For Berkshire Hathaway Energy, which was once considered the crown jewel of the non-insurance businesses, and is currently navigating a period of heightened risk. Operating earnings stood at $3.98B, which is a modest increase but still overshadowed by massive contingent liabilities. The main one being the large liability exposure related to the wildfires in the western part of the U.S., mainly affecting the PacificCorp subsidiary.

Though despite the challenges, BHE continues to expand its industrial footprint with the completion of the deal for OxyChem, the chemical arm of Occidental Petroleum. This acquisition fits the Berkshire model for acquiring businesses with regulated or contracted cash flows and significant asset bases. The move will further integrate it with Berkshire’s energy portfolio, which includes the 27% stake in Oxy (OXY) as well as the 6.5% stake in Chevron (CVX).

Valuation

Valuing Berkshire on financial metrics or compared to peers is not really possible given its one-of-a-kind status in terms of companies. That’s why I did an SOTP, DCF, and scenario-based analysis. On an SOTP view, the total equity value, taking into account the private businesses, the cash position, and the equity investment portfolio, would amount to $1.15T, or $797,450 per Class A share and $531.63 per Class B share, or an 11.3% upside. Though I’ll say that in a scenario-based analysis, even the bear case only amounts to a slim downside of -0.5%, giving a certain floor to the stock price. On a probability-weighted Class B price, this would imply a stock price of $537.27, still a fair upside from here in twelve months' time.

On a DCF front, though, my total per Class B share would come to $428.87, which would be a 10.2% downside, but Berkshire is a staple of protection in case of a downturn in the economy. Though many could argue that Berkshire, due to its size, is now a proxy for the overall economy, so it might be hard to grow at my assumed CAGR.

There are also certain developments I'm closely looking at, as there is a growing speculation that Berkshire may eventually use its cash hoard to repurchase the massive block of shares held by Buffett’s estate after they donated to his foundation. This would prevent a massive overhang of shares on the market and serve as the largest repurchase in corporate history. I’ll also look forward to the Japanese expansion as BRK continues to deepen its ties to the Japanese economy, as the five major trading houses are now seen as the fourth-pillar holdings comparable in importance to Apple and American Express (AXP).

Risks

The Buffett premium has now largely evaporated, as quantified by the compression in the price-to-book ratio from 1.75x to 1.5x, representing roughly $150B in lost market value attributable to the leadership transition. That was the main point in my past analysis, and now we still don’t know how the new stewardship will be able to regain such a premium and if it can replicate the market-moving influence the firm once had.

There are also the key challenges that subsidiaries are facing with BNSF Railway, as well as the Insurance Underwriting, in which Greg Abel has warned will continue to see headwinds during the year.

Conclusion

I’ll say that the core dynamic of the firm remains intact, with the insurance float providing an unparalleled source of low-cost capital, while the industrial side provides a resilient cash flow base. But the challenge arises from the leadership transition needing to prove to the market that it can push Berkshire into a new successful era without the Oracle of Omaha. I still want to wait and see if it is worth buying shares at this point. I keep my Hold rating on the firm.

data.title: This Rally Won't End Well
data.description:
Thesis Summary

The S&P 500 is pushing back towards 7,000, and a lot of investors are finding this very surprising.

Oil is up 60% YTD, the Iran conflict is ongoing, inflation is heating up, and there’s little help expected to come from the Fed.

So what’s going on exactly?

In this article, I’ll discuss why I believe the market has been so resilient over the last few weeks.

Following this, we’ll dive into exactly why this might be the time you want to take some chips off the table and specifically how you could implement this through options.

Why Are Markets Not Down?

This is an intriguing question, and one we can’t really answer with 100% certainty. We can, however, make some educated guesses.

Positioning And Flows

The thing about markets is that they don’t always move in the direction you’d expect based on headlines, and that’s often due to positioning.

In this particular scenario, the Iran war headlines created a situation in which market participants were very well hedged.

This is reflected in the increase in Short ETF Volume.

When there is so much shorting, market makers have to hedge these short positions, which actually makes them go long.

And of course, if the price does eventually move up, this can then be accelerated even further by a short squeeze.

Positioning also ties into market flows, which have also created a situation where CTAs are underweight the market, and due to specific price signals, they have to buy into the market.

On the flow side, Goldman models Commodity Trading Advisors as buyers of roughly $45B in U.S. equities over the next week, with approximately $34B of that concentrated in the S&P.

Source: SA Press Release

These CTAs also add fuel to the fire because they have to chase rallies once momentum shifts.

Market Liquidity

Liquidity is another factor to take into account. Tax refunds from the BBB are going into consumer accounts, and that’s creating a tailwind for the economy.

Since the Iran conflict began, there’s an argument to be made that $700 billion of stimulus-like liquidity has supported the system.

Once we add tax refunds, government spending, and also the slight expansion of the Fed balance sheet.

AI Acceleration

Lastly, the war has done little to stop the popularity of the AI trade, which we can’t deny is being supported by the data.

Demand seems to be increasing across the board. GPU prices are up, there’s not enough memory, and the hyperscalers continue to roll out more investment.

Pair this with the fact that valuations for a lot of the Mag 7 were becoming quite attractive, and you have a perfect recipe for buyers to step in.

Why I Expect A Further Pull-Back

But all of this doesn’t mean the market isn’t going to roll over further. There’s a lot of good reasons to expect weakness.

War Escalation

While peace talks are ongoing, the geopolitical risk remains, and we continue to see escalation on the ground and sea.

The US has moved to blockade Iranian ports, and the latter has now threatened to retaliate if this continues.

And while oil prices have come off their highs, it does seem like they are stabilizing at near $100, which is bad news if they stay at these levels.

Inflation

Inflation is now the highest it has been in two years, and this is going to be challenging for stocks.

If we get a couple more hot prints, then tech stocks, especially, are going to get hit hard.

Seasonality

Let’s also not forget that this is an election year. Midterms come with added volatility, and seasonality data also shows that historically, this is the worst time in terms of market performance.

Earnings Volatility

We’ve also seen this play out before during previous earnings cycles.

We rally into earnings, and even though most companies beat, we see big sell-offs across the board and increased volatility.

I think a lot of companies are going to get hit hard this earnings season, too, and give back a lot of their recent gains.

How To Hedge With A Put Spread

So, how do we play this?

Personally, I still want to stay invested in a lot of the AI names, and I also think accumulating Mag 7 at these lower valuations is a good move.

However, I think at the current level in the VIX, a put spread could be used to hedge the downside risk.

For example, you could buy a slightly out-of-the-money put, around 5% below today's levels, and then sell a deeper out-of-the-money put, perhaps somewhere in the 10% correction level.

This structure keeps your cost around ~1% of notional value and will act as insurance.

The key is that you’re not just betting on price falling; you’re also positioned to benefit if volatility spikes, which seems like a likely scenario.

If we do indeed see a mild correction of 5%, you could realistically see a 2–3x return on that premium, while a sharper 10% drawdown and a VIX spike into the 25–35 range could push returns closer to 5–6x.

A 5% sell-off would put us back at the 200-day EMA, while a 10% sell-off would essentially be a revisit of the previous lows.

Both are key areas you want to watch.

Of course, if nothing happens and the market grinds higher, you simply lose the premium, but that’s the cost of hedging.

Final Thoughts

Ultimately, I think the next two to three months could hold significant downside, and today’s price and VIX give us a good entry to put some shorts on and hedge our portfolio.

data.title: H&R Block Q3 Preview: Price Correction Is A Blessing For The Long-Term Investor
data.description:
H&R Block (HRB) will be reporting one of the most crucial earnings quarters on May 6th (estimated). That is where most of the money will be made, as it will cover most of the tax season. I wanted to take a look at the expectations in more detail, and seeing that the company’s share price is significantly down since my last article over a year ago, I thought it would be a good time to revisit to see if the company is a buy at these levels now.

What to Expect

So, for earnings, analysts are expecting the company to make around $5.63 and $5.77 on a GAAP and adjusted basis, respectively, on around $2.34B in revenues. This translates to around 6% growth in GAAP earnings and around 3% growth in sales. Nothing spectacular, but I am not expecting anything big from a tax enterprise.

Unfortunately, the company didn’t provide any concrete guidance for the upcoming quarter, but they did reaffirm fiscal 2026 guidance after the last report. The company kept its full-year outlook at $3.875-$3.895B in revenue and $4.85-$5.00 in adjusted EPS. The best way to get a comparison is to see what the analysts are expecting the company’s full-year performance to be. As we can see below, estimates are largely in line with the management.

And what about the upcoming quarter and whether the company will beat or miss estimates on this crucial tax season quarter? We can see that, over the last ten quarters, the company beat revenues 90% of the time and beat EPS 80% of the time. Regarding Q3s, the company hasn’t missed once.

It seems that analysts are consistently underestimating HRB’s performance, but even that did not help the company’s share price over the last year, as it is down around 50%.

I expect the company to beat on both the top and bottom lines because the company is entering the seasonally important quarter with a favorable setup. With the company just posting a Q2 revenue beat and reaffirming its full-year guidance, I am expecting a surprise to the upside.

What I’m looking for

The company’s Wave segment has been showing consistently decent performance over the last while. This small-business financial software helps HRB diversify its revenue streams, and I am expecting to see decent performance going forward. Last quarter, Wave grew around 12%. But let’s be honest, no one is going to pay attention to the smaller revenue segments in Q3 because they are not the ones to be bringing in all the dough this time around. Sure, the smaller segments are great when the company is just surviving the boring seasons of no activity. The day-to-day of Q1 and Q2. All eyes will be on the company’s assisted tax preparation, US royalties, US DIY tax preparation, and Refund transfers. These segments come back to life, but no other segment compares to the assisted tax preparation. In Q3 ’25, this segment grew over 6.5%. I think that is quite a decent growth number, so anything below that, I would think there are some growth concerns and would need to get to hear the comments from the management on why that was the case.

Also, I would like to hear some comments regarding the progress of its Horizons transformation plan that was introduced all the way back in 2020. It’s been around 6 years since then, and yes, there has been some activity and push to become a bit more than a seasonal tax preparer, but the new segments are still very low compared to the juggernaut that is the assisted tax prep. I would like to hear what other acquisitions the company is looking to make, to expand its reach, and to make the boring non-tax seasons look a lot better.

It also seems like the company’s share price got hit by the fear of AI disruption. I am having a hard time believing that the company’s valuation was cut in half because of its fundamentals. Earnings will continue to grow at least double what its top-line growth will be, and that is a sign of a very healthy situation. I don’t ever expect anything in terms of top-line performance, but as long as the company becomes more profitable and efficient, I will be the first one to buy such a company, even if it seems boring.

The real reason for such a devastating blow to its valuation is the fear of AI and how AI is going to change how tax preparation workflows are managed. It seems that the Street thinks HRB will be left behind if it doesn’t adapt to the new technology. I agree with that sentiment to an extent; however, I don’t see how autonomous AI is going to replace humans in such important work, with so many regulations, and real repercussions if something is not filed correctly. There is no way I would rather let some AI agent do my tax returns, no matter how much I hate doing such paperwork. The technology, although impressive, cannot handle such big tasks without some sort of hallucination. I use AI agents essentially every day, and there is not a single time that I take its word at face value. So, the whole SaaSpocalypse, I think, was a massive overreaction, at least right now, and that means there were a lot of companies like HRB that got hit hard even though the fundamental picture is intact.

Of course, HRB is not going to fall victim to the ever-changing dynamic of technology today. It already offers AI Tax Assist, which is designed to make DIY filing easier by giving 24/7 tax help. Its AI was trained on tax scenarios, tested by many experts, and built around client trust. Augmentation is what I am looking for when it comes to tax filings, not replacement. I’d like to hear more such initiatives from the management.

Is it a good time to buy HRB before earnings?

I like to look at a valuation model to decide whether it is or not. Below are the assumptions I used.

I think the company is very easy to model, since I don’t expect any sort of surprises in terms of performance, whether that is going to be on the top line or profitability. Here, I went with 3% CAGR, as that is what analysts are assuming as well (more or less). For margins, 21.5% is what gives us the $5 of EPS for the next year, and then I just gradually increased it by just a little bit. Analysts see double-digit growth in earnings, but I am being more conservative here. The rest I kept as close to the company’s historical averages as I could. Also, for my discount rate, I went with 10%, as is standard for me, instead of the company’s low estimate of around 6%. That way, I am being even more conservative, as that provides quite a decent margin of safety. Also, I lowered my terminal growth rate to 1.5% instead of my usual 2.5%. So, with these estimates, we get the following projections.

As you can see, nothing fancy, just a solid mid-single-digit growth. Very achievable in the long run. And with these projections, we see that HRB’s intrinsic value is around $56.62 per share, which means the company is trading at a very attractive discount to its fair value.

Risks and Closing Comments

The AI fears will continue to apply pressure on the company’s share, as the sentiment continues to worsen, undeservedly so, might I add. The macroeconomic uncertainty will most likely play a huge role in the US economy in the future. The current administration is too unpredictable at times, which introduces an extra layer of volatility. The company might never recover because it will be seen as a legacy tax service provider, and the competition that may be more advanced and efficient will take its lunch. That is a possibility, but a far-fetched one in my opinion.

I think if you have already been holding HRB in your portfolio, I don’t see why you wouldn’t pick up some more shares at this price. If you bought it at a much higher price than now, I think you should like it a lot more now, especially if you believe that the whole AI disruption narrative is overdone, as I do. Therefore, I believe at these prices, the risk-to-reward is quite appealing, so I am upgrading the company to a buy.

data.title: Dutch Bros: De-Risking Ahead Of Earnings As Competition Heats Up (Downgrade)
data.description:
We're at an incredibly volatile juncture in the stock markets. Despite an unclear resolution to the conflict in Iran, plus a choppy macroeconomy with weak consumer spending, the S&P 500 has shot back up near all-time highs, reversing all of its earlier losses. Software stocks and consumer-oriented retail and restaurant names continue to see large downsides, while semiconductor and energy shares reap the majority of this year's gains. Stock selection is crucial, especially as we move into a potentially volatile Q1 earnings season.

Dutch Bros (BROS), in my view, will see a bit of vulnerability with its recent rebound rally. The stock is down ~15% since the start of the year, but so far investors have been generally pleased with its growth performance. The question now is, can Dutch Bros keep on top of consensus expectations, as the competitive winds may be shifting against the company's favor?

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Dutch Bros in March, when the stock was trading in the mid-$40s. In the space of a month, Dutch Bros has managed an impressive ~10% rebound rally. At the same time, I think there are a number of factors that have moved in the unfavorable direction for this stock, prompting me to cut my rating on the stock to "Neutral."

The next major catalyst for Dutch Bros is its Q1 earnings release, due on May 6. In my view, the stock is ripe for profit-taking ahead of that date.

All eyes on McDonald's as it steps up its drinks game

Since inception, the discussion around Dutch Bros has always been centered around how its comp sales trends are outpacing the major giant in the coffee industry: Starbucks (SBUX). But as Starbucks struggles and focuses on retaining its coffeehouse vibe through new CEO Brian Niccol's "Back to Starbucks" plan, increasingly it looks like Dutch Bros' real competition is McDonald's.

McDonald's has been an unbelievably bright spot in an inflation-pressured restaurant sector. Whereas most large restaurant chains have issues decelerating comp sales trends, McDonald's comp sales (which measure the growth from existing restaurant locations, excluding the contribution from newly opened stores) recently accelerated to 6.8% y/y in Q4, accelerating 440bps versus just 2.4% growth in Q3.

The company has credited its comps turnaround in the United States to its focus on value menu offerings, particularly its nationally advertised $5 Meal Deal.

Recently, McDonald's made a major announcement that shifts its major focus to drinks. The company is releasing a new lineup of energy drinks and custom sodas, and it's planning specifically on pricing these products at a low price to undercut Starbucks (SBUX) and Dutch Bros, per a report from the Wall Street Journal. McDonald's core aim is to bring in new customers outside core mealtimes and improve the slower day parts.

Dutch Bros' success in recent quarters hasn't just been due to coffee but also due to customers driving in for energy drinks in the middle of the day.

Already, on Dutch Bros' recent Q4 earnings call, the company was pushed on McDonald's running energy drink promotions in test markets. Dutch Bros CEO Christine Barone responded by minimizing the notion of competition, noting that Dutch Bros is one of the core innovators in the space.

So on the first question on the energy test, as we shared last quarter, we really didn't see anything in our business. I think we are the category creator of customized energy. And I think anything that's being shared about the category that creates new customers and new customer interest, I would guess that we would likely benefit from that. And so we do feel really confident about what we're doing from an energy perspective. We've been in the energy business for a very long time and really know what customers want in this space.

However, we think that McDonald's deeper entry into the energy drink category in Q2, and at deeper price cuts, will represent a fresh competitive threat for Dutch Bros. We note as well that one of Dutch Bros' core advantages versus Starbucks is the convenience of its drive-through lane: an advantage it doesn't have over McDonald's.

Q1 look-ahead

As previously mentioned, Dutch Bros is due to report Q1 earnings on May 6. I believe this will be a negative catalyst for the company. The key items to watch out for are:

  • Decelerating comp sales, owing in part to the run-up in gas prices. As a reminder, Dutch Bros has guided to 3-5% systemwide comp sales in FY26, versus 5.6% in FY25 as a whole and 7.7% in Q4. Already, Dutch Bros faces a tough comp in FY25, especially as it laps pricing increases. Even before we consider the impact of McDonald's (which will be more of a Q2 issue), we think there is likely to be under-recognized impact from soaring gas prices. Not only is this cutting into consumers' discretionary budgets, but many people may be pulling back on the frequency of their driving overall. For a company like Dutch Bros that relies so heavily on drive-through for its business, we don't expect that skyrocketing gas prices are good news.
  • Weakening shop contribution margins. Already as Dutch Bros was exiting Q4, the company was facing a slight ~1-point y/y decline in shop contribution profit margins to 27.6%. The company attributed this to a rise in coffee costs, and we don't expect much respite as we continue to see reports on elevated green coffee prices.
  • Comments around pricing power and competition. We do think there is a large chance that Dutch Bros will acknowledge the stiffer competition from peers like McDonald's in Q1, and be forced to run more frequent and deeper promotions to maintain market share among value-oriented consumers. This will have the impact of blunting average ticket and comp sales trends.

Valuation and key takeaways

Lastly, we also think Dutch Bros' recent rebound rally from February lows in the mid-$40s leaves the stock ripe for profit taking. Trading at 21x forward adjusted EBITDA, the company is trading much richer than McDonald's as well as direct rival Black Rock Coffee Bar, which is achieving stronger comp sales growth versus Dutch Bros.

We think there's a high likelihood of Dutch Bros' multiple re-rating back down as the competition narrative heats up and comp sales fade. Take the opportunity before earnings to de-risk your position in this stock.
